role_summary: Saint Augustine, Florida beautician and civil rights movement participant; recruited by Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (catalog entry 103) and Martin Luther King Jr. into the early-1960s kneel-ins at segregated St. Augustine churches; member of the Scott Street protective network that guarded Hayling's home; lead organizer and fundraiser for the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument commemorating the local civil rights movement.
ai_reading: Counting how often each interview surfaces across Vickers's per-passage top-neighbor lists, three voices tie for first at twenty appearances each, and all three are St. Augustine: the joint Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer interview (entry 4), Robert Bagner Hayling (entry 103), and Purcell Maurice Conway (entry 99); Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51) follows at fourteen and Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96) at nine. On her opening passage the single highest cosine matches are Duncan (0.93), Hamilton and Ulmer (0.92), and Hayling (0.91), all recorded in the same September 2011 St. Augustine session. The cluster is the tightest single-city grouping in the audited subset of the catalog: the embedding reconstructs the St. Augustine cohort as a unit, foregrounding local participants whom the national civil-rights narrative records only in passing. Two axis readings cut against the cultural framing. On the Southern Struggle versus Northern Struggle axis she sits at only -0.19 toward Southern, a faint reading for a participant in a deep-South Florida campaign, because a large share of her testimony recounts her World War II migration to a New York shipyard and to Boeing in Seattle, language the axis weights toward the Northern pole. On the Nonviolence as Theology versus Armed Self-Defense axis she reads near the center at +0.50, only slightly toward nonviolence, even though her testimony documents the armed West Side network that posted men in the trees around Hayling's house and a telephone warning chain for Klan night-rides; the embedding does not register that defensive material as strongly armed-self-defense, plausibly because she narrates it as fear and neighborly protection rather than as Black Power praxis.
biographical_paragraph: Barbara Edna Vickers's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. The external record of her life runs through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid and the city of St. Augustine's civil-rights archive [src: 1, src: 2]. Born in 1923 in St. Augustine, Florida, she attended the St. Benedict Catholic parochial school through eighth grade and then Excelsior High School, at the time the only high school for Black students in the city [src: 1, src: 2]. After graduating around 1942 she left for New York, where she trained as a tack welder and worked in a shipyard through World War II before moving on to wartime aircraft work at Boeing in Seattle [src: 1]. She returned to St. Augustine in the late 1950s after marrying A.D. Vickers, a St. Augustine man, and opened a home beauty salon, later serving as president of the local beauticians' association [src: 1]. When Dr. Robert Bagner Hayling (catalog entry 103, the NAACP Youth Council adviser and Florida SCLC figure whose organizing brought Martin Luther King Jr. to the city in 1964) and King recruited volunteers, she joined the kneel-ins at segregated St. Augustine churches and belonged to the Scott Street network of neighbors who guarded Hayling's home against Klan night-rides [src: 1]. Years afterward she led the fundraising drive that built the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument, the city's memorial to the local civil rights movement [src: 2]. Her testimony is held by the Library of Congress as entry 5 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Barbara Edna Vickers
interview_snippets.lead_in: The night Martin Luther King Jr. came to St. Augustine, Hayling told him he needed people to carry the kneel-ins to the white churches. Vickers recalls being singled out from the pews:
interview_snippets.quote: Dr. King looked at me in the church of all people. And he said, young lady, will you go? And I said, yes, and I thought to myself, what did I say?

interview_snippets.speaker: Barbara Edna Vickers
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the neighborhood defense system that grew up around Hayling's home, the armed West Side men in the trees and the telephone chain that warned of night-rides:
interview_snippets.quote: So those boys were there. And we formed this little network that when the clans came through House Street, we would call each other and let each other know.

interview_snippets.speaker: Barbara Edna Vickers
interview_snippets.lead_in: Her grandmother, who lived under the older terror, asked why the young people were provoking white St. Augustine. Vickers answered:
interview_snippets.quote: I said, we don't want to eat with them. We want to be free. We want to be able to go to the restaurant, pay our money and have the food, not taking it out the back door.

interview_snippets.speaker: Barbara Edna Vickers
interview_snippets.lead_in: Decades later, when Hayling urged her to settle for a marker rather than a full monument to the foot soldiers, she refused:
interview_snippets.quote: I said no. I want a monument. Do you know what the head and tails? Yes. Hard work. But we're going to get a monument.

interview_snippets.speaker: Purcell Maurice Conway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Fellow St. Augustine resident Purcell Maurice Conway names Vickers, his neighbor, when crediting the people who built the city's civil rights memorials:
interview_snippets.quote: And I like to say again, my neighbor again, Mrs. Barbara Vickers. I recall when she took on her loan test of trying to make a monument for the foot soldiers.

interview_snippets.speaker: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recorded the same day in the same St. Augustine session, the joint Hamilton and Ulmer interview names Vickers among the few local figures who refused to let the city erase its civil rights past:
interview_snippets.quote: Anything that they could get rid of, concerning the civil right movement, they got rid of until seven people in this town like Barbara Vickers, Dr. R.B. Hayling.

movement_context: The St. Augustine campaign Vickers took part in was the SCLC's last major direct-action campaign before the July 2, 1964 signing of the Civil Rights Act, and the historiographical record credits the St. Augustine campaign with helping break the Senate filibuster that had held the bill in committee through the spring of 1964 [src: 3]. The campaign's national-attention moments included the June 11, 1964 arrest of Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph Abernathy (catalog page available) at the segregated Monson Motor Lodge restaurant on the St. Augustine bayfront, and the June 18, 1964 "swim-in" at the same property when Monson Motor Lodge owner James Brock poured muriatic acid into the swimming pool while integrated swimmers were in it (an action photographed by the Associated Press's Horace Cort and frequently cited in the historiography among the images that intensified national pressure during the Senate cloture fight over the bill) [src: 3, 4]. The kneel-ins Vickers joined were a Florida-east-coast extension of the SCLC tactical pattern from the 1961-1962 Albany, Georgia and 1963 Birmingham, Alabama campaigns, in which sustained nonviolent presence at segregated churches and commercial facilities produced a photographic record that shaped congressional opinion on the pending legislation [src: 4]. The local commemorative organization that grew out of the campaign, ACCORD (the St. Augustine Forty Years of Civil Rights movement), is named in the same St. Augustine interviews as a custodian of that history; Purcell Maurice Conway (entry 99) references its dedications alongside the Foot Soldiers Monument [src: 1].
legacy_and_reception: The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument Vickers raised funds for was dedicated May 14, 2011 in the city's Plaza de la Constitucion, the same plaza where the 1964 demonstrations had been held, with thirty-one foot soldiers' names inscribed on the central plaque [src: 2]. Her oral history was recorded a few months later, on September 13, 2011, by interviewer Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, with videographer John Bishop, at a bayfront hotel in St. Augustine; Hayling was recorded in the same session [src: 1]. The interview was conducted for the Civil Rights History Project, the joint undertaking of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture [src: 1]. The entry was reviewed across the project's multi-pass audit cascade [src: 5].

gallery.caption: St. Augustine civil rights demonstrations, 1964, from the Florida Memory project of the State Library and Archives of Florida. The footage documents demonstrators at Butler Beach, integrated occupation of city streets, and intervention by the Florida Highway Patrol during the spring 1964 SCLC direct-action campaign Vickers co-organized.
gallery.photographer: State Library and Archives of Florida (Florida Memory project)

gallery.alt: St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque, dedicated May 14, 2011
gallery.caption: The St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument plaque in the Plaza de la Constitucion, dedicated May 14, 2011. Vickers led the fundraising drive for the monument; thirty-one foot soldiers' names are inscribed on the central plaque, commemorating participants in the 1960s civil rights protests in St. Augustine.
